questions.q: The syllabus dot-point asks you to "account for the similarities and differences" between paths in four field situations. What does this verb require?
options: Four separate descriptions, one per scenario
A comparison that gives the reasons and cause-and-effect, not just descriptions
Only the equations for each case
A single diagram with no explanation
questions.explanation: "Account for" means give the reasons and explain cause and effect, and the question demands a comparison, not four descriptions glued together. Writing four separate paragraphs caps low (band four) because it contains no comparison statements.

questions.q: Which single question determines whether a particle's path is a parabola or a circle?
options: Whether the particle is positive or negative
Whether the field is strong or weak
Whether the force stays fixed in direction or stays perpendicular to the velocity
Whether the particle is heavy or light
questions.explanation: A force fixed in direction (uniform gravity, uniform electric field) gives a parabola and does work; a force always perpendicular to the velocity (magnetic force, circular-orbit gravity) gives a circle and does no work. This single distinction is the engine of the whole comparison.

questions.q: Why does a satellite in a circular orbit have constant speed, even though gravity is acting on it?
options: Gravity is too weak to do work in space
In a circular orbit gravity is perpendicular to the velocity, so it does no work
The satellite is in a region with no gravitational field
The satellite's engines maintain its speed
questions.explanation: In a circular orbit gravity points to the centre while the velocity is tangential — perpendicular. A force perpendicular to the velocity does no work, so the speed and kinetic energy stay constant. (In an elliptical orbit gravity has a component along the motion, so the speed varies.)

questions.q: Why does the syllabus list "masses in uniform gravitational fields" and "satellites in orbit" as separate cases?
options: Because satellites are heavier than projectiles
Because a satellite's field is radial and non-uniform, giving a circle, while a uniform field gives a parabola
Because satellites experience no gravity
Because the two cases use different values of \(g\) but behave identically
questions.explanation: A projectile moves across a uniform gravitational field (constant force, fixed direction) and traces a parabola. A satellite moves through a radial field that points inward and weakens with distance, acting as a centripetal force and giving a circle (or ellipse). The behaviours differ, so they are listed separately.

questions.q: In which two of the four scenarios does the field do work on the particle?
options: Uniform gravity and uniform electric field
Circular orbit and uniform magnetic field
Uniform electric field and uniform magnetic field
Circular orbit and uniform gravity
questions.explanation: In uniform gravity and a uniform electric field the force is fixed in direction, so when the particle moves along the field, work is done and kinetic energy changes. In a circular orbit and a uniform magnetic field the force stays perpendicular to the velocity, so no work is done and the speed is constant.

questions.q: Why is a satellite's circular orbit said to resemble a magnetic circle more than a gravitational parabola?
options: Both involve charged particles
In both, the force is perpendicular to the velocity, doing no work and giving a circle
Both forces fall off as one over distance squared
Both require the particle to be moving at the speed of light
questions.explanation: Forgetting where the force comes from, both have a force perpendicular to the velocity, giving the same three consequences: no work, constant speed, circular path. The geometry sets the path; only the origin of the force differs (gravity vs the magnetic interaction).

questions.q: An electron accelerated from rest through 100 V reaches \(5.9\times10^{6}\ \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\), then enters a 6 mT field perpendicular to its motion. What happens to its speed in the magnetic field?
options: It increases, because the magnetic field adds energy
It decreases, because the field opposes the motion
It stays at \(5.9\times10^{6}\ \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\), because the magnetic field does no work
It drops to zero as the electron circles
questions.explanation: The magnetic force is perpendicular to the velocity and does no work, so the speed is unchanged at \(5.9\times10^{6}\ \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\). The field only bends the electron into a circle (here of radius about 5.6 mm). Contrast: the electric field did work and increased the speed.

questions.q: How does an electric field differ from a gravitational field in the kind of force it can exert?
options: An electric field only attracts, like gravity
An electric field can attract or repel depending on the sign of the charge, whereas gravity only attracts
An electric field cannot do work, but gravity can
An electric field acts only on moving charges
questions.explanation: Both forces are constant in a uniform field and independent of velocity, but the electric force points with the field for a positive charge and against it for a negative charge — so it can attract or repel. Gravity is always attractive. Both, however, do work and give parabolas.

questions.q: What is the single most common structural mistake on the "compare the paths" extended response?
options: Using too many equations
Answering field by field instead of by comparison point
Mentioning the word "whereas"
Including a diagram
questions.explanation: Writing one paragraph per field produces correct descriptions but no comparison, so it caps low. The mark-earning structure organises by comparison axis — running all scenarios across each axis — and uses linking words like "whereas" and "in contrast".

questions.q: What common feature unifies all four scenarios despite their differences?
options: All four produce parabolic paths
In every case the force is the net force, so Newton's second law applies to all four
All four forces do work on the particle
All four require the particle to be charged
questions.explanation: In each scenario the relevant force is the net force on the particle, so \(F = ma\) applies without exception. The differences live only in the force law itself and the direction of that force relative to the velocity; everything else follows from those two.
